coredata.dc:title: Surface engineering and concentration-dependent emission activated flexible tunable fluorescence from lignin-based N-doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Xiaohui
Yang, Rendang
Wang, Yang
Ni, Shuzhen
Cheng, Chen
Sheng, Jie
coredata.dc:description: Achieving the flexible and tunable fluorescence of lignin-based N-doped carbon dots (LCDs) remains a win–win yet challenging strategy. In this study, combining the surface engineering and concentration regulation strategy enabled the flexible adjustment of LCDs fluorescence emission from blue to yellow regions (345 to 560 nm). The surface electron-donor –OH groups not only enhanced the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) to 14.75 % but activated the spectral shift of reduced LCDs (RLCDs). These arose from the reinforced π-electron conjugation and generated new –OH-related extrinsic defects with elevating concentration. Conversely, the electron-withdrawing CO groups participated in the blue-to-green fluorescence modulation. Still, they drastically reduced the PLQY of oxidized LCDs (OLCDs) to 2.13 %, due to the larger particle size and more non-radiative transfer induced by CO groups. Based on these, CDs/PVA films and printed fluorescent patterns with multicolor fluorescence were realized for anti-counterfeiting, and the RLCDs@Al2O3 hybrid facilitated the precise fluorescence fingerprint tracking and recognition. This study provided a facile strategy for enriching and developing novel LCDs with flexible fluorescence for their advanced applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignin-based carbon dots
Fluorescence tunability
Surface engineering
Concentration dependence
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent anti-counterfeiting by screen printing ink
Analysis of PL emission properties of CDs
Effect of surface chemical structure on PL emission

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of CDs preparation and their applications in anti-counterfeiting.

(a–c) TEM images (inset: HRTEM image), (d–f) size distribution, (g–h) AFM images (inset: height profile with black line), and (j–l) height distributions of LCDs, RLCDs, and OLCDs at a concentration of 0.02 mg·mL−1.

Normalized PL emission spectra of (a) LCDs, (b) RLCDs, and (c) OLCDs solutions with concentrations of 0.02, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, and 0.5 mg·mL−1 and (d–f) their CIE color coordinates at their optimal excitation.
(a–c) XPS full-spectra, (d–f) C1s, (g–i) N1s, (j–l) O1s high-resolution XPS spectra of LCDs, RLCDs and OLCDs.
(a–c) UV–vis absorption spectra, and (d–f) Tauc plots of LCDs, RLCDs and OLCDs with different concentrations. (g–i) Schematic diagram of PL emission mechanism for LCDs, RLCDs and OLCDs.
DFT models, their HOMO/LUMO energy levels and band gaps at the B3LYP/6-31G (d, p) level in water solvent atmosphere.

(a) Photographs of (i) LCDs@PVA, (ii) RLCDs@PVA and (iii) OLCDs@PVA films under sunlight and UV light (before and after 30 days). (b) Photographs of fluorescent patterns on a polypropylene paper substrate daylight and UV light (365 nm) for LCDs, RLCDs, and OLCDs. Stability evaluations of the fluorescent patterns based on RLCDs: soaked in different solvent conditions, and exposed to UV light (365 nm). (c) Schematic illustrating the structure of RLCDs@Al2O3, images of RLCDs@Al2O3 powders designing “I ♡ CD” pattern, stamp ink, and fingerprint made of RLCDs@Al2O3 powders under daylight (left) and 365 nm light (right).

head.ce:title: Surface engineering and concentration-dependent emission activated flexible tunable fluorescence from lignin-based N-doped carbon dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Achieving the flexible and tunable fluorescence of lignin-based N-doped carbon dots (LCDs) remains a win–win yet challenging strategy. In this study, combining the surface engineering and concentration regulation strategy enabled the flexible adjustment of LCDs fluorescence emission from blue to yellow regions (345 to 560 nm). The surface electron-donor –OH groups not only enhanced the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) to 14.75 % but activated the spectral shift of reduced LCDs (RLCDs). These arose from the reinforced π-electron conjugation and generated new –OH-related extrinsic defects with elevating concentration. Conversely, the electron-withdrawing CO groups participated in the blue-to-green fluorescence modulation. Still, they drastically reduced the PLQY of oxidized LCDs (OLCDs) to 2.13 %, due to the larger particle size and more non-radiative transfer induced by CO groups. Based on these, CDs/PVA films and printed fluorescent patterns with multicolor fluorescence were realized for anti-counterfeiting, and the RLCDs@Al2O3 hybrid facilitated the precise fluorescence fingerprint tracking and recognition. This study provided a facile strategy for enriching and developing novel LCDs with flexible fluorescence for their advanced applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), an excellent photoluminescent 0D nanomaterial, are garnering growing interest, depending on their exceptional benefits of highly stable photoluminescence (PL), adjustable fluorescent color, and facile functionalization [1]. These superiorities render CDs promising candidates for versatile applications [2–6]. With the increased demand for sustainable development and carbon neutrality, lignin-based carbon dots (LCDs), have been continuously developed. Lignin is one of the abundant renewable aromatic biomacromolecules [7,8], accounting for 15–30 wt% of lignocellulosic biomass, and consisting of methoxylated phenylpropanoid subunits [9]. The rich O-containing groups and π-π conjugations in lignin [10] make it similar to CDs in structure and suitable for CDs preparation [11–13]. However, lignin is just considered a biomass byproduct, being undervalued and burned for heat energy in the pulping industry [14]. Converting lignin to CDs is viewed as a “two-birds-one-stone” strategy for the high-value utilization and clean development of CDs. Currently, detaching the PL emission of LCDs from the blue-green region encounters some bottlenecks [15]. And the uncertainties in the multi-color fluorescence mechanism impede the colorful PL performances and application prospects of LCDs.

The tunable PL emissions of CDs can also be attributed to the results of the quantum confinement effect (QCE) from the conjugated sp2 domain in the carbon core and surface defects from the edge of CDs [16,17]. Many strategies including heteroatom doping [8,18,19], size control [20], surface modification [12], and concentration tunability [21] have been accepted for improvement of PL performances. Concentration regulation of single CDs is a straightforward and effective strategy for multiple PL emissions. In typical studies, Wang et al. [18] reported that the obtained N-doped CDs exhibited multicolor PL emission from blue to red by adjusting concentration due to the intense intermolecular interactions. Zhang et al. [22] achieved tunable blue to yellow PL emission in CDs, along with a transition from aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) to aggregation-induced emission (AIE) with elevating concentration, based on the Förster energy transfer between single and aggregated CDs particles. In addition, the concentration-dependent PL emission of the obtained CDs was related to the synergy of reduction of band gap and the content of C-O-C/C-O-H groups [23]. The increase in CDs concentration can increase the aggregation degree and particle size, corresponding to a long emission wavelength, similar to the size-dependent PL emission of CDs [24]. The C-O-C/C-O-H groups grafted on the sp2 hybridized carbon structure could generate O-related excited states within the n-π* gap [25,26], and then change the PL emission with concentration regulation. Therefore, both the integral carbon nucleation and the attached surface chemical groups contribute to the concentration-dependent PL features of CDs during the agglomeration process. Besides, the effects of surface groups and their interactions on the concentration-dependent PL properties of LCDs have been tentatively investigated [4,12]. However, the comprehensive studies on the fluorescence regulation mechanisms governing the concentration dependence and surface chemistry, are still less and limited. The precise mechanism of internal structure and different surface chemical structures significantly impact their concentration-dependent PL properties, which can offer some significant insights into fluorescence tunability and enhancement.

In this study, we report a flexible tunable fluorescent N-doped LCDs obtained by combined surface engineering and concentration regulation. The reduced LCDs (RLCDs) grafted by –OH groups and oxidized LCDs (OLCDs) with CO groups were designed by surface chemical modifications. Based on the comprehensive analysis of morphology, chemical structure, optical behaviors, and density functional theory (DFT) calculation, the fluorescence regulation mechanism behind surface electron-donating/withdrawing chemistry and concentration-dependent emission of CDs were probed. Especially, the interactions of intrinsic sp2 conjugated domain, –OH and –CO groups were explored in the agglomeration process by adjusting concentration. The blue-to-yellow fluorescence of RLCDs was realized due to the synergy of enhanced π electron conjugated system and –OH related external electronic states with elevating concentration. The diminished blue-to-green fluorescence of OLCDs came from the larger particle size and increased non-radiative transitions by CO groups. In addition, the obtained potential fluorescent films, invisible CDs printed inks, and CDs@Al2O3 hybrid with stable optical traits were fabricated for anti-counterfeiting and fingerprint tracking and recognition, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . This study offers guidance for rational design and fluorescence enhancement of LCDs, advancing their potential utility in anti-counterfeiting technologies.

Poplar wood chips were derived from the State Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Engineering (Guangzhou, China). Anhydrous alcohol (≥99.7 %) was acquired from Titian Scientific Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). O-phenylenediamine (98 %) (OPD) was obtained from Aladin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Polyvinyl alcohol 124 (PVA 124) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent CO., Ltd. (Guangzhou, China). Sodium borohydride (NaBH4), aluminum hydroxide (Al(OH)3), and sodium periodate (NaIO4) were purchased from FUCHEN Chemical Reagent CO., Ltd. (Guangzhou, China) and Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), respectively. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were obtained from Guangzhou Reagent CO., Ltd. (Guangzhou, China).

The hydrolyzed lignin (Mw = 1214, Mm = 828, polydispersity index = 1.47) was first extracted from the poplar hydrolyzed liquor as in our previous study [4]. The preparation and purification steps were described in Fig. S1. 0.1 g hydrolyzed lignin and 0.2 g oPD were used as carbon and nitrogen precursors, respectively. 20 mL anhydrous alcohol (≥99.7 %) solvent with a low boiling point acted as the reaction solvent to promote the carbonization and polymerization of precursors [27]. The mixture was then transferred to a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE)-lined stainless steel reactor, ultrasonicated (800 W) for 10 min to ensure uniform dispersion, and then oven-heated at 180℃ for 10 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, the reacted LCDs solution was rotary evaporated to remove the residual ethanol, and re-dispersed in 20 mL of deionized water. The obtained LCDs aqueous solution was centrifuged for 5 min to obtain the supernatant from the solids. The supernatant was filtered through 0.22 μm organic membrane followed by dialyzes against deionized water using a 500 Da dialysis bag for removing the fluorescent small molecules (below 1 nm) from LCDs. Finally, the above solution was concentrated via rotary evaporation and freeze-dried to yield LCDs powder.

0.1 g LCDs and 0.2 g NaBH4 were dissolved in 100 mL deionized water and stirred overnight at room temperature for reduction. Subsequently, the solution was purified using an identical process to obtain the reduced LCDs (RLCDs)

0.1 g LCDs and 0.2 g NaIO4 were dissolved in 100 mL deionized water, and the HCl (2 mg·mL−1) was added to adjust pH to 3. The mixture was stirred for 24 h under dark conditions at room temperature for oxidation. Afterwards, the reaction was quenched by isopropanol. Finally, the oxidized LCDs (OLCDs) were collected after purification.

A 5 % PVA transparent solution was first prepared by dissolving PVA at 95 °C with stirring. LCDs, RLCDs, or OLCDs powders were uniformly dispersed in the above PVA solution under stirring at room temperature to obtain the CDs/PVA solution (LCDs: 0.02 mg·mL−1, RLCDs: 0.5 mg·mL−1 or OLCDs: 0.5 mg·mL−1). CDs/PVA solutions were then dropped into a plastic mold and dried at 40 °C to obtain the CDs/PVA fluorescent films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent anti-counterfeiting by screen printing ink
ce:section.ce:para: The above CDs/PVA solutions were stirred at 45 °C for 2h to prepare the fluorescent inks, which were subsequently employed to screen painting for anti-counterfeiting.

2.5 mg RLCDs and 0.5 g Al(OH)3 were mixed well in 5 mL deionized water under stirring at room temperature. The mixture was oven-dried at 100 °C for 6h to obtain RLCDs@Al(OH)3 powders. Then, RLCDs@Al2O3 powders were prepared via calcination at 600 °C for 2h under a nitrogen atmosphere, named RLCDs@Al2O3.

CDs morphologies were examined using Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM 2100F, JEOL, Japan) operating at 200 kV acceleration. Size and height distributions were measured using a Zetasizer Nano (ZS90, Malvern, UK) and an atomic force microscope (AFM, SPM-9700, Shimadzu, Japan), respectively. The morphologies and elemental composition of CDs/PVA membranes were analyzed by a Scanning electron microscope (SEM, Quanta FEG 250, Hillsboro, OR, USA) equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) instrument. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was recorded on an X-ray diffractometer (D8-ADVANCE, Bruker, Germany) with Cu Kα X-ray radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) in the range of 10–80°. A Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, TENSOR27, Bruker, Germany) with potassium bromide (KBr) pellets was used to elucidate the chemical groups in samples ranging from 400 to 4000 cm−1 with 16 scans at 4 cm−1 spectral resolution. The PL emission spectra of CDs were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Scientific Fluoromax-4, HORIBA, USA) at 2 nm slit widths of excitation and emission lights. Photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) of samples were determined using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Scientific Fluoromax-4+, HORIBA, USA), repeated three times. Decay curves were detected by a fluorescence lifetime imaging microscope (SMS, HORIBA, USA). An ultraviolet (UV) visible spectrophotometer (UV-1900, SHIMADZU, Japan) was used to measure the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs. Thermalgravimetric (TG) coupled with differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) analysis were measured using a synchronous thermal analyzer (STA449F3, NETZSCH, Germany). Details of density functional theory (DFT) calculation: all geometries of models were optimized and calculated at the B3LYP/6-31G (d, p) level using the Gaussian 09 program in the aqueous phase. The molecular orbitals were subsequently visualized with Gauss view 05 software.

The TEM micrographs and size distributions of CDs clearly illustrated the uniform, monodispersed, and quasi-spherical feature of LCDs, RLCDs, and OLCDs (Fig. 2 a–c), with the average diameters of 3.10, 2.83 and 3.48 nm, respectively (Fig. 2d–f). The HRTEM images exhibited a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphene [7,18,28,29] (Fig. 2a–c, inset). The downsize of RLCDs indicated that LCDs may be cleaved by NaBH4 reduction but without altering the graphite structure of LCDs [12]. The AFM images in Fig. 2g-l demonstrated the topographic morphology of monodispersed CDs, the heights of CDs were 0.5–2.0 nm with an average value of 0.73, 0.75, and 0.91 nm, respectively. Variations in the morphology of three CDs with different concentrations were also investigated to clarify the aggregation behaviors (Fig. S2-S3). LCDs gradually enlarged from 3.32 nm to 7.72 nm (ranging from 0.05 to 0.2 mg·mL−1), and severely aggregated at 0.5 mg·mL−1. RLCDs were uniformly dispersed with ∼2.8 nm size at 0.05–0.2 mg·mL−1, yet stacked and indistinguishable at 0.5 mg·mL−1 due to the small molecular distance [30]. The increase in OLCDs size was most pronounced, suggesting their severe aggregation behavior. Similarly, the increase in concentration also caused the higher heights of CDs (Fig. S3). Moreover, the size distributions of CDs from Dynamic light scattering (DLS) also determined the aggregation behaviors of CDs (Fig. S4), which was consistent with the TEM and AFM results. The DLS results were larger than those by TEM owing to the solvatation layer around CDs solution. The differing aggregation behaviors of the three CDs demonstrated their diverse compositions and prompted investigations into their physicochemical structural characteristics.

The XRD pattern of CDs (Fig. S5a) showed a broad diffraction peak at 22.3–23.7°, distinct from that of lignin (21.6°), indicating the nanoscale dimension of CDs induced by the carbonization of lignin [4,31]. The d spacing values of LCDs, RLCDs, and OLCDs were 4.06, 3.97, and 3.83 Å, respectively, distinguishing from that of graphite (3.44 Å), attributing to the diverse grafted surface chemical substituents, such as –OH, –NH2 and –CO groups. The FT IR spectra (Fig. S5b) revealed the surface functional groups of the three CDs. The stretching vibration of the O-H/NH2 group (3430 cm−1) and C-H groups (2940 and 2840 cm−1) was observed in both lignin and the three CDs. The stretching vibration peak of non-conjugated CO in ketone, aromatic nucleus with CO, aromatic nucleus, C-H in alkyl, C-O and C-O-C bonds were observed at 1714, 1614, 1514, 1458, 1215, and 1116 cm−1, respectively, deriving from the nature of lignin. The peaks assigned to CC/CN (1620 cm−1) [32], CO (1508 cm−1), C-N (1388 cm−1), and C-O (1049 cm−1) appeared in three CDs. In summary, the obtained LCDs, RLCDs, and OLCDs can be inferred as an aromatic carbon skeleton functionalized with diverse O- and N-containing chemical groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of PL emission properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: PL emissive properties of the three LCDs with the same concentration gradients were investigated in Fig. 3 and Fig. S6-S8. The diverse excitation-dependent emission of three CDs (Fig. S6-S8) indicated that not only the quantum size but also the surface defects contributed to the fluorescence emission of CDs. The PL emission intensities of CDs were compared as RLCDs > LCDs > OLCDs. For LCDs, the PL emission wavelength was located at ∼354 nm with a weak secondary peak at 410 nm at a concentration of 0.02 mg·mL−1. At 0.1 mg·mL−1, the secondary peak was getting stronger and reached the maximum intensity value. But the redshifted PL peak was then decreased and their CIE color coordinate was transformed from (0.17, 0.11) to (0.19, 0.17) (Fig. 3a,d) along with the incremental particle size of LCDs (Fig. 1). This may be related to the size-dependent PL emission properties, i.e., the large sizes correlate with the longer emission wavelength [23]. Additionally, the generated new electronic states from some energy levels within the n-π* transition also contributed to the redshift of PL emission [22,23]. In Fig. 3b, the PL peak position (∼345 nm) and intensity of RLCDs remained unchanged over the 0.02 to 0.2 mg·mL−1 concentration range (Fig. 2 and Fig. S5). It suggested the similar energy levels in RLCDs solutions even though the concentration increased, related to the close size of RLCDs. While the peak was substantially redshifted to 560 nm with the corresponding color coordinate transforming from (0.18, 0.11) to (0.48, 0.49) at 0.5 mg·mL−1 (Fig. 3e). It was due to the narrow spacing of RLCDs, enhancing their molecular interactions [30], and then forming more new energy levels for PL emission. In Fig. 3c, the PL emission peak of OLCDs was located at 365 nm, accompanied by a faint second peak at 405 nm at 0.02 mg·mL−1. With increasing concentration to 0.1 mg·mL−1, the second peak redshifted and raised to the maximum intensity. At 0.5 mg·mL−1, the variable emission peak migrated to 512 nm and their color coordinates shifted from (0.15, 0.05) to (0.28, 0.37). As seen, the concentration-dependent PL emission of OLCDs may be rigged by the QCE, i.e., size-dependent emission.

Herein, alterations in the surface structure of CDs lead to changes in aggregation behaviors, the appearance of different multiple PL peaks, and their shifts, thereby resulting in the new emission properties of the three CDs. The increased –OH groups in RLCDs and CO groups in OLCDs inevitably produced more new energy levels with increasing concentrations. Based on our results, the substantial redshift PL concentration-dependent emission of RLCDs possibly arises from the combined results of the intrinsic sp2 conjugated domain and the –OH-related surface energy traps. The PL emission migration of OLCDs may be attributed to the comprehensive impacts of increasing particle size and CO relevant emission states as the concentration increases. These findings approve a preliminary basis for establishing a clear correlation between the chemical structure (sp2 conjugated domain in carbon core and surface chemical groups) and the concentration-dependent fluorescence of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of surface chemical structure on PL emission
ce:section.ce:para: The XPS analysis was conducted to analyze the chemical compositions on the surface of LCDs, RLCDs, and OLCDs (Fig. 4 ). The notable three peaks at 284.8, 399.9 and 532.4 eV detected in survey scans of the three CDs corresponded to C1s, N1s, and O1s elements, respectively (Fig. 4a–c). The O/C ratio in LCDs, RLCDs, and OLCDs was calculated as 51.4 %, 39.4 %, and 53.1 %, respectively, suggesting that the NaBH4 reduction and NaIO4 oxidation treatments successfully changed the oxidation degree of LCDs. The high-resolution spectra of C1s (Fig. 4d–f) showed three distinguish peaks at 284.8, 286.3–286.5, and 288.4–288.9 eV, corresponding to CC/C-C, C-O/C-N, and CO groups, respectively [33]. The corresponding proportions of each functional group are listed in Table S1. Due to the reduction of CO groups, the ratio of (C-O/C-N)/CC and C-O/CO in RLCDs was up to 38.91 % and 235.8 %, respectively. Instead, the CO/CC ratio in OLCDs rose to 10.39 %, and this was accompanied by a reduction in the C-O/CO ratio (30.6 %) because of the oxidation of C-O groups. These intuitively showed the apparent differences in surface oxidation degree, i.e., OLCDs > LCDs > RLCDs. In Fig. 4g–i, the –N, C-N, and N-H bonds appeared at 399.1–399.2 eV, 399.7 eV, and 400.4–400.7 eV, respectively [34], especially, the –NO2 peak signal at 407.6 eV [7] was detected in OLCDs after the oxidation. Nevertheless, the N1s spectra were not used as a primary reference owing to the low N content in CDs. The O1s high-resolution spectra could be resolved into two peaks, at 531.9–532.2 and 533.0–533.2 eV, that can be assigned to CO and C-O groups, respectively [35] (Fig. 4j–l). The area ratio of the C-O group surged to 70.22 % in RLCDs and plummeted to 23.45 % in OLCDs. Accordingly, the ratio of C-O/CO significantly increased to 235.8 % in RLCDs, and decreased to 30.6 % in OLCDs, indicating that numerous –OH groups are present in RLCDs, while OLCDs are almost occupied by CO groups. It can be seen that the O-containing groups with different types and numbers on CDs undoubtedly affect their charge distributions, and aggregation behaviors and then tune their PL emission features.

The PLQYs results are shown in Table S2. As seen, the PLQY of RLCDs increased to 14.75 % with the augmentation of –OH groups. Electron-donating –OH groups can combine with CDs aromatic rings to generate strong p-π conjugation and thus intensify the PL emission [36]. Conversely, the OLCDs exhibited a PLQY of only 2.13 %, attributed to the non-radiative relaxation processes induced by surface CO groups [37]. In Fig. S9, the fluorescence decay curves of three CDs at 0.02 mg·mL−1 are well fitted by a biexponential function, producing relaxation with two lifetimes of τ1 and τ2. The weights of each lifetime component for three CDs are listed in Table S3. The fast τ1 and slow τ2 belonged to the radiative recombination of intrinsic states (π-π*) and different surface defects (n-π*), respectively. The τavg of dispersed LCDs, RLCDs and OLCDs were 4.57, 4.10, and 4.90 ns, respectively. The ratio of τ1 raised from 38.00 % in LCDs to 52.85 % in RLCDs. This is because the n electron in the –OH group outside the aromatic conjugated structure can be excited and thus results in the formation of new OH-related surface excited states. Furthermore, the n-π electron can be conjugated with the aromatic carbon core, which significantly expands and enhances their π-electron conjugated system [12]. Therefore, the CDs with excellent multicolor fluorescence performances can be expected by the synergy of intrinsic eigenstates and surface emitting states from surface electron-donating groups via concentration regulation and surface engineering.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs with different concentrations were also investigated to further reveal the PL emission mechanisms. As depicted in Fig. 5 a–c, the strong absorption peak centered at 250–300 nm was derived from π-π* transitions because of CC bonds in the sp2 conjugated domains [7]. The shoulder peak at 300–400 nm arose from the n-π* transitions associated with surface CO or conjugated C-N/CN groups [20]. The weak peak at 400–500 nm was assigned to the transitions from surface electronically excited defects to intrinsic states. As the concentration increased to 0.5 mg·mL−1, the enhanced absorption in the 250–400 nm was attributed to the variable optical energy gaps, arising from the intrinsic sp2 conjugated structure and enriched surface fluorescent substituents.

The optical band gap (Eg) of CDs solutions can be calculated from the Tauc plot (i.e., the curve of (αhν)2 versus hν converted from UV–vis absorption spectra), where α, h and ν is the absorption coefficient, Plank constant (6.63 × 10−34 J) and the light frequency, respectively [38]. The Eg values of CDs at varying concentrations were determined by measuring the x-axis intercept of the extension from the linear part of the curves. In Fig. 5d–f, the Eg of the three CDs were reduced as the concentration increased from 0.02 to 0.5 mg·mL−1, as shown in Table S4. The PL emission wavelengths of LCDs, RLCDs, and OLCDs redshifted by 76, 214, and 147 nm, respectively, and their Eg values were narrowed by 2.19, 2.19, and 2.00 eV, respectively, as depicted in Fig. S10. Therefore, –OH and CO groups endow CDs with fluorescence emission tunability by generating a series of new intrinsic absorption bandgaps and O-containing related surface traps. The introduction of –OH groups effectively facilitates the CDs fluorescence tunability by expanding the π electron conjugated structure of the aromatic domain, and the resulting enhanced p-π conjugation with a double-bond system [12]. According to the PL properties analysis, the concentration-dependent blue-to-navy blue, blue-to-yellowish, and blue-to-greenish PL emission of LCDs, RLCDs, and OLCDs were attributed to the electronic transitions from HOMO to LUMO, as shown in Fig. 5g–i. Under their optimal excitation, the excited electrons and holes would reach their Eg from their HOMO and LUMO levels. Their related excited states would be replenished, facilitating the radiative transition and fluorescence emission. In Table S6, some methods for multi-color PL emission of CDs, have been explored by tuning parameters, such as raw materials, reaction conditions, or dopants, but are cumbersome and elaborate. Compared to the above, we reported a simple strategy based on the concentration regulation of the single RLCDs from natural lignin. Additionally, there were not many toxic chemicals and reagents used in our synthetic process. The present method is both environmentally friendly and low-cost.

The energy differences (δE) between the π and π* orbitals of LCDs, RLCDs, and OLCDs was below 1.5 eV within the triplet carbene ground state [20]. The δE of LCDs, RLCDs, and OLCDs at 0.02 mg·mL−1 was 0.5, 0.42, and 0.6 eV, respectively, and changed to 0.66, 0.55 and 0.68 eV, respectively, with the elevated concentrations. These indicated that the increase of electron-donating substituents, such as –OH, tends to induce a small δE which favors the triplet state of CDs. In contrast, the electron-withdrawing substituents can stabilize σ nonbonding orbital which inclines towards singlet state [39]. We further confirm that PL emission tunability can be achieved through the concentration-dependent PL emission activated by surface chemical engineering, specifically by incorporating electronic-donating groups, such as –OH groups.

The DFT theoretical calculations were performed to explore the fluorescence emission mechanism of CDs with different chemical structures in water solvent. The designed models, their HOMO/LUMO energy levels, and band gaps were presented in Fig. 6 . CDs molecules with pure 7 and 14 aromatic rings were first designed to study the intrinsic states. As the number of benzene rings increased from 7 to 14, the LUMO energy significantly increased, while the HOMO energy decreased marginally, meanwhile, the band gap diminished from 4.03 to 0.45 eV, indicating that the enlargement of the sp2 conjugated structure contributes to narrowing the energy gap, in agreement with the QCE. As –OH groups were fused with 7 aromatic rings, the HOMO energy decreased slightly and the band gap narrowed from 4.03 to 3.82 and 3.62 eV, respectively. In contrast, the presence of the –COOH group led to a dramatic reduction of HOMO energy and a band gap of merely 0.39 eV. The interactions between the multiple chemical substituents and their effects on PL emission tunability were also assessed. When the –COOH group was grafted to the model (7 benzene rings with a –OH group), the LUMO energy rose and the band gap decreased to 0.34 eV. Besides, the band gap remained at 0.37 eV with the increase of –OH groups from 1 to 3. The interactions between –OH and N-related groups were also crucial for the fluorescence properties of CDs due to the implication of N doping. When the C-N-C or N-(C)3 group was added, the band gap narrowed to 3.70 and 2.20 eV, respectively. The addition of –OH, C-N-C, N-(C)3, and –NH2 groups maintains the band gap at 2.03 and 2.00 eV, respectively, without a noticeable decline versus the cohesive effects of –OH and N-(C)3 groups. Therefore, the large sp2 conjugated domain, CO, and N-(C)3 groups facilitate the long wavelength emission of CDs, and the antagonistic effect between –OH and CO groups was clarified. In our study, the overlapping RLCDs in water solution possess eigenstate within π-π* gaps and ample –OH-related electronic states, which collectively enhance the yellow fluorescence of RLCDs. In addition, the –OH groups efficiently suppress the nonradiative process and promote the high PLQY of RLCDs. Nonetheless, the energy transitions including nonradiative recombination processes within n-π* gaps intensify owing to the formation of the –CO group, resulting in poor fluorescence and low PLQY in OLCDs. Thus, it is confirmed that the electron-donating groups grafted on CDs can synergize with the sp2 conjugated system in the carbon core to improve fluorescence as well as color tunability.

The luminescence anti-counterfeiting technology, especially, fluorescent anti-counterfeiting, has garnered growing attention due to the excellent optical stability and controllability of CDs [40,41]. Continuous advanced anti-counterfeiting strategies should be developed to combat rampant counterfeiting. In this study, the application performances of three CDs in fluorescent films and printing inks were explored, leveraging the diverse concentration-dependent PL emission of the three CDs (Fig. 7 ). As shown in Fig. 7a, the LCDs, RLCDs, and OLCDs were encapsulated in a PVA substrate to prepare solid-state CDs/PVA films with high clarity. Under 365 nm excitation, the films emitted bright blue, yellowish, and greenish fluorescence, respectively, attributable to the fluorescent emission of the internal LCDs, RLCDs, and OLCDs under the encapsulation of PVA [42] (as detailed in section 3.2). The PL emission spectra of films were further investigated. In Fig. S11, films exhibited distinctive excitation-dependent fluorescence emission characterization, and the RLCDs@PVA film had the strongest PL intensity. With the BaSO4 integrating sphere measurement, the PLQY of LCDs@PVA, RLCDs@PVA, and OLCDs@PVA film was calculated as 1.12, 2.36 and 0.78 %, respectively (Table S5), consistent with the result of CDs in Table S2. The presence of N content in the SEM images (Fig. S12-S14) of CDs/PVA films indicated the effective intervention and uniform dispersion of the three CDs within the PVA matrix. Besides, the fluorescent films still emitted robust fluorescence after exposure to sunlight for 30 days, showcasing their surprising photobleaching resistance and potential in photoconversion applications.

The transparent CDs/PVA printing inks were used to screen printing on a polypropylene paper substrate (Fig. 7b). The printed substrate did not display any information in daylight. While, under UV (365 nm) light, the moon, cloud patterns as well as the “Carbon dots” characters with increased information complexity, featuring blue, yellowish, and greenish fluorescence, respectively, were showcased. The patterns based on RLCDs still exhibited the optimized fluorescence due to the cooperative interaction between the sp2 conjugated domain in the carbon core and surface OH-related surface defects. While that of OLCDs had a weak fluorescence induced by the hindrance of –CO groups. The printed fluorescent cloud pattern exhibited exceptional solvent resistance, without fluorescence decay to the naked eye. The “Carbon dots” fluorescent characters still had bright fluorescence even after prolonged exposure to the UV light for 1 to 30 days. These results demonstrated the great potential of the obtained RLCDs/PVA ink in information encryption and decryption, and security marking.

To realize the solid-state fluorescent materials for anti-counterfeiting, a small amount of RLCDs was embedded into the Al(OH)3 substrate. The RLCDs@Al(OH)3 could be transformed into RLCDs@Al2O3 with a tighter structure after calcination at 600℃ as demonstrated by XRD and TG-DSC analysis (Fig. S15). The schematic of RLCDs@Al2O3 structure was plotted in Fig. 7c. In Fig. S16, the pure Al2O3 powder has no fluorescence at 365 nm excitation, but RLCDs@Al2O3 powders emitted blue fluorescence at 410 nm with a PLQY of 0.84 % due to the internal loading of RLCDs. Moreover, the TEM images of RLCDs@Al2O3 displayed a layered nanosheet structure (Fig. S17a), and the lattice fringes of RLCDs were also detected (Fig. S17b). The presence of C, and N elements in the XPS result of RLCDs@Al2O3, further demonstrates the successful embedding of RLCDs (Fig. S18). Surprisingly, four designed patterns (I ♡ CD) based on RLCDs@Al2O3 powders exhibited bright blue fluorescence, indicating their great potential application in anti-counterfeiting. Finally, the RLCDs@Al2O3 powders were used as stamp ink for fingerprint tracking and recognition. The texture of the fingerprint could be clearly discriminated by the naked eyes under UV light radiation. These results show that the obtained RLCDs@Al2O3 hybrid possessed a great capacity for fingerprint tracking and recognition.

Single lignin-based N-doped CDs with tunable blue to yellow fluorescence are designed, based on a simple surface engineering and concentration regulation strategy. To explore the effect of surface chemistry on the aggregation behavior with concentration regulation, the surface structure of LCDs was modified into electron-donating/absorbing types. The tunable blue to yellow fluorescence of RLCDs via increasing concentrations originated from the energy transitions within the small sp2 conjugated structure, the synergy of enhanced π electron conjugation, and multiple OH-related surface states, respectively. In contrast, electron-withdrawing CO groups cause the decreased blue-to-green fluorescence of OLCDs due to the larger sp2 conjugated structure and CO related electronic energy states, inducing the increased nonradiative transitions by them. By using the tunable fluorescence of CDs, fluorescent anti-counterfeiting materials were prepared. LCDs-, RLCDs-, and OLCDs-based films exhibited blue, yellowish, and greenish fluorescence, respectively, with great sunlight resistance. Besides, printed anti-counterfeiting patterns on substrates were obtained using the CDs-based invisible inks, and anti-counterfeiting patterns were visible and stable even under various solvent and UV lamp aging conditions. The obtained RLCDs@Al2O3 hybrid also had excellent potential in precise fingerprint tracking and recognition. This work provides a simple LCDs fluorescence modulation method for their potential anti-counterfeiting applications.

Xiaohui Guo: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Rendang Yang: Resources, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yang Wang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Shuzhen Ni: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Software. Chen Cheng: Validation, Data curation. Jie Sheng: Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2023A1515110431) and Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2024A1515011654).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.155146.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
